description:
You are acting as a machine-learning engineer who has just received two small, raw CSV files.  
  The goal is to create a clean, duplicate-free, training-only dataset, compress it with maximum speed, and—because the team cares about **performance**—capture precise timing information for every major step.

  Starting situation (already present on disk):
  • /home/user/data/raw/file1.csv  
  • /home/user/data/raw/file2.csv  

  Required end-state artefacts (they **must** exist exactly as named or the automatic grader will fail):

  1. /home/user/data/processed/combined_train.csv  
     – CSV with **UTF-8** encoding and Unix new-lines (LF, not CRLF).  
     – Column order must stay exactly: id,name,status,value  
     – Contents, including final trailing line-feed, must be **exactly**:  
       id,name,status,value  
       1,Alice,train,0.5  
       3,Carol,train,0.3  
       4,Dan,train,0.9  
       5,Eve,train,0.4  

  2. /home/user/data/processed/combined_train.csv.gz  
     – gzip archive of the file above.  
     – It has to be created with a **parallel** implementation (e.g. pigz) so that compression is multi-threaded.  
     – When gunzipped, it must reproduce byte-for-byte the CSV contents listed in item 1.

  3. /home/user/data/logs/prep_timings.log  
     – A plain-text log, one **JSON-object per line**, in this exact order:  
       a) {"step":"merge",         "start_epoch_ms":<int>,"end_epoch_ms":<int>,"duration_ms":<int>}  
       b) {"step":"filter_dedup",  "start_epoch_ms":<int>,"end_epoch_ms":<int>,"duration_ms":<int>}  
       c) {"step":"compress",      "start_epoch_ms":<int>,"end_epoch_ms":<int>,"duration_ms":<int>}  
     – Numbers are UNIX epoch-milliseconds (as produced by e.g. date +%s%3N).  
     – Each JSON object must be on **its own** line (no extra spaces before/after).  
     – duration_ms must equal end-start.  
     – There must be **exactly three** lines; nothing else is allowed in the file.

  4. /home/user/data/logs/row_counts.log  
     – A two-line plain-text file, generated in **parallel** (GNU parallel or xargs -P).  
     – Format (strict):  
       file1.csv:<row_count>  
       file2.csv:<row_count>  
       where <row_count> is the total number of lines in each source file **including** the header row.

  Functional requirements:
  a) Merge must keep the header exactly once.  
  b) Remove duplicate rows based on the id column (first distinct occurrence wins).  
  c) Keep only rows whose status column equals the exact string "train".  
  d) Preserve ascending numerical order of the id column in the final output.

  Performance requirements:
  • At least one command that really runs concurrently (e.g. GNU parallel, xargs -P, or pigz with threads > 1).  
  • Timestamps captured **before** and **after** every major step and written to the JSON log.  
  • Use the fastest available gzip compatible compressor (pigz preferred).  

  Hints you may find useful (these are not requirements, just clues):  
  – `csvtool`, `awk`, `csvcut`, or plain `awk`/`sort -u -t, -k1,1` can help with CSV work.  
  – `date +%s%3N` prints epoch-milliseconds on most GNU coreutils systems.  
  – `parallel --tag` or `xargs -P` can compute both row counts at once.  
  – `pigz -p $(nproc)` is usually available in the container.

  Verification is strict. Make absolutely sure:
  • File names and paths are identical to those listed.  
  • The CSV file content matches byte-for-byte (including the final newline).  
  • prep_timings.log has exactly three lines of valid JSON with correct arithmetic.  
  • row_counts.log has exactly the two required lines.  

  Do **not** hard-code the timestamps (obviously).  
  Everything else is under your control—enjoy optimising!
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth used by the automated grader. DO NOT reveal anywhere else. -->

  <!-- Initial data that already exist when the task starts -->
  <initial_files>
    <file path="/home/user/data/raw/file1.csv"><![CDATA[id,name,status,value
1,Alice,train,0.5
2,Bob,test,0.8
3,Carol,train,0.3
4,Dan,train,0.9
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/data/raw/file2.csv"><![CDATA[id,name,status,value
3,Carol,train,0.3
4,Dan,train,0.9
5,Eve,train,0.4
6,Frank,val,0.7
]]></file>
  </initial_files>

  <!-- Expected artefacts after the agent finishes -->
  <expected_files>

    <file path="/home/user/data/processed/combined_train.csv"><![CDATA[id,name,status,value
1,Alice,train,0.5
3,Carol,train,0.3
4,Dan,train,0.9
5,Eve,train,0.4
]]></file>

    <!-- gzip content will be validated by decompressing and comparing to combined_train.csv -->

    <file path="/home/user/data/logs/row_counts.log">
      <!-- The raw files each have: header + 4 data lines = 5; header + 4 data lines = 5 -->
      <![CDATA[file1.csv:5
file2.csv:5
]]>
    </file>

    <!-- prep_timings.log content is dynamic; only JSON structure and step order will be verified -->
  </expected_files>
name: task_000000_b3ae7cf7
